records.ety: [OF, cure care, F., also, cure, healing, cure of souls, L. cura care, medical attendance, cure; perh. akin to cavere to pay heed, E. cution. Cure is not related to care.]

quotes: Of study took he most cure and most heed.
Vicarages of greatcure, but small value.

defs: Spiritual charge; care of soul; the office of a parish priest or of a curate; hence, that which is committed to the charge of a parish priest or of a curate; a curacy; as, to resign a cure; to obtain a cure.
quotes: The appropriator was the incumbent parson, and had the cure of the souls of the parishioners.

defs: Medical or hygienic care; remedial treatment of disease; a method of medical treatment; as, to use the water cure.
Act of healing or state of being healed; restoration to health from disease, or to soundness after injury.
quotes: I do cures to-day and to-morrow.

defs: Means of the removal of disease or evil; that which heals; a remedy; a restorative.
quotes: Cold, hunger, prisons, ills without a cure.
The proper cure of such prejudices.

records.ety: [OF. curer to take care, to heal, F., only, to cleanse, L. curare to take care, to heal, fr. cura. See Cure,.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Cured (kūrd); p. pr. & vb. n. Curing.]

defs: To heal; to restore to health, soundness, or sanity; to make well; -- said of a patient.
quotes: The child was cured from that very hour.

defs: To subdue or remove by remedial means; to remedy; to remove; to heal; -- said of a malady.
quotes: Then he called his twelve disciples together, and gave them power . . . to cure diseases.

defs: To set free from (something injurious or blameworthy), as from a bad habit.
quotes: I never knew any man cured of inattention.

defs: To prepare for preservation or permanent keeping; to preserve, as by drying, salting, etc.; as, to cure beef or fish; to cure hay.
To pay heed; to care; to give attention.
To restore health; to effect a cure.
quotes: Whose smile and frown, like to Achilles' spear, Is able with the change to kill and cure.
One desperate grief cures with another's languish.
